title: Parse two unsigned integers and write their integer quotient, or error on any failure
prompt: Read two lines from standard input. Each line contains an unsigned decimal integer that fits in u32 (range 0 to 4294967295 inclusive). Parse both. If either parse fails (empty line, non-digit characters, leading sign, leading zero is OK, value exceeds u32 max), write the literal string `error\n` to standard output and exit. If both parse and the second value is 0, write `error\n` to standard output and exit. Otherwise compute the integer floor quotient `a / b` and write the result as decimal followed by a single newline. Do not write to standard error. Exit with status 0 in every case. Trailing whitespace on the digit lines is tolerated (trimmed); a stray leading `+` is not accepted as valid.

input_schema.stdin: two lines: dividend a (u32), divisor b (u32)

output_schema.stdout: either `<decimal_quotient>\n` on success or `error\n` on any failure

notes_input_convention: Zero reads a from argv[1] and b from argv[2] because Zero 0.1.2 has no exposed stdin capability; the values are interpreted identically to the two stdin lines other languages read (trailing whitespace trimmed). All other languages read two newline-terminated lines from stdin. This task opens the error-handling arc: three independent failure modes (parse-a, parse-b, divide-by-zero) all collapse to the same `error\n` output. Zero exercises its `Maybe<T>` parse shape via `std.parse.parseU32`; other languages use their idiomatic parse-or-fail primitive.
